sections.title: Listening
sections.description: 40 questions • Approximately 30 minutes • You will hear each recording only once

parts.title: Part 1: Discussion about economic development paradigms
parts.instructions: Listen to the conversation and answer questions 1-10.

script.narrator:
Good morning. This is the International English Language Testing System, IELTS. You will hear a number of different recordings and you will have to answer questions on what you hear. There will be time for you to read the instructions and questions and you will have a chance to check your work. All the recordings will be played once only. The test is in four parts. At the end of the test you will be given ten minutes to transfer your answers to an answer sheet.

Now turn to Part 1. You will hear a conversation between a development economist and a policy advisor about evolving economic development paradigms. Look at questions 1 to 10. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 10.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: The conventional Washington Consensus approach to development has demonstrated significant limitations in addressing the complex challenges facing emerging economies. Its emphasis on liberalization, privatization, and fiscal austerity failed to account for institutional quality, human capital development, and technological capabilities.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Indeed, Professor. The post-Washington Consensus acknowledges these shortcomings but still operates within a fundamentally neoliberal framework. What's needed is a more heterodox approach that recognizes the role of industrial policy, strategic integration into global value chains, and building domestic innovation systems.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Precisely. The success of East Asian economies demonstrates the importance of sequenced liberalization combined with active industrial policy. However, simply replicating these models is insufficient—each economy requires context-specific strategies based on its unique institutional and technological capabilities.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: The concept of 'product space' developed by Hausmann and Hidalgo provides valuable insights here. Economies evolve by moving to products that are closely related to their existing productive capabilities. This path dependence creates both opportunities and constraints for structural transformation.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Excellent point. This underscores the importance of strategic diversification rather than random industrial promotion. The challenge lies in identifying and removing binding constraints while building complementary capabilities in human capital, infrastructure, and institutional governance.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: The digital transformation adds another layer of complexity. Developing countries face the dual challenge of building traditional industrial capabilities while simultaneously preparing for the digital economy. This requires investments in both physical and digital infrastructure.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Moreover, the environmental imperative necessitates green industrial policies. The traditional development path of 'grow first, clean up later' is no longer viable. Countries must pursue sustainable structural transformation from the outset.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: This implies rethinking international development cooperation as well. Rather than prescribing one-size-fits-all solutions, development partners should support countries in developing their own diagnostic capabilities and implementing context-appropriate strategies.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: The role of the state remains crucial but must evolve. Instead of picking winners, the state should facilitate discovery processes, coordinate complementary investments, and ensure inclusive outcomes. This requires building state capacity and combating rent-seeking.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Ultimately, successful development strategies must balance global integration with domestic capability building, economic efficiency with social inclusion, and short-term pragmatism with long-term strategic vision.

questions.questionText: What has demonstrated limitations according to the speakers?
questions.translation: Điều gì đã thể hiện những hạn chế theo các diễn giả?

questions.questionText: What failed to account for institutional quality?
questions.translation: Điều gì đã không tính đến chất lượng thể chế?

questions.questionText: What does the post-Washington Consensus still operate within?
questions.translation: Đồng thuận Hậu Washington vẫn hoạt động trong khuôn khổ nào?

questions.questionText: What demonstrates the importance of sequenced liberalization?
questions.translation: Điều gì chứng minh tầm quan trọng của tự do hóa theo trình tự?

questions.questionText: What concept provides insights about economic evolution?
questions.translation: Khái niệm nào cung cấp hiểu biết về tiến hóa kinh tế?

questions.questionText: What creates both opportunities and constraints?
questions.translation: Điều gì tạo ra cả cơ hội và ràng buộc?

questions.questionText: What dual challenge do developing countries face?
questions.translation: Các nước đang phát triển phải đối mặt với thách thức kép nào?

questions.questionText: What development path is no longer viable?
options: A) Grow first, clean up later
questions.translation: Con đường phát triển nào không còn khả thi?

questions.questionText: What should the state facilitate instead of picking winners?
questions.translation: Nhà nước nên tạo điều kiện cho điều gì thay vì chọn người chiến thắng?

questions.questionText: What must development strategies balance?
options: A) Global integration and domestic capability
questions.translation: Các chiến lược phát triển phải cân bằng điều gì?

parts.title: Part 2: Lecture on platform economics and digital markets
parts.instructions: Listen to the lecture and answer questions 11-20.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 2. You will hear a lecture on platform economics and digital markets. Look at questions 11 to 20. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 20.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: The emergence of digital platforms represents one of the most significant economic transformations of our time, creating new market structures that challenge conventional economic theory. Platform businesses exhibit distinctive characteristics including network effects, multi-sided markets, and data-driven value creation. These features create winner-take-most dynamics that can lead to rapid market concentration. The economics of platforms fundamentally differs from traditional pipeline businesses—value is created through ecosystem coordination rather than linear production. This creates novel competition policy challenges, as market definition becomes ambiguous and competitive dynamics operate across traditionally separate sectors. Data accumulation and algorithmic pricing enable sophisticated price discrimination and personalized offerings, raising concerns about consumer welfare and market fairness. The regulatory framework for digital platforms remains underdeveloped, struggling to balance innovation promotion with competition preservation. Jurisdictional boundaries are increasingly irrelevant in digital markets, requiring international coordination in competition policy. The future evolution of platform economics will likely involve greater attention to data governance, platform cooperativism, and algorithmic accountability as societies grapple with the implications of these powerful new market structures.

questions.questionText: What represents significant economic transformation?
questions.translation: Điều gì đại diện cho sự chuyển đổi kinh tế quan trọng?

questions.questionText: What distinctive characteristics do platforms exhibit?
questions.translation: Nền tảng thể hiện những đặc điểm khác biệt nào?

questions.questionText: What dynamics can lead to market concentration?
questions.translation: Động lực nào có thể dẫn đến tập trung thị trường?

questions.questionText: How does platform value creation differ?
questions.translation: Tạo giá trị nền tảng khác biệt như thế nào?

questions.questionText: What becomes ambiguous in platform competition?
questions.translation: Điều gì trở nên mơ hồ trong cạnh tranh nền tảng?

questions.questionText: What enables sophisticated price discrimination?
questions.translation: Điều gì cho phép phân biệt giá tinh vi?

questions.questionText: What remains underdeveloped?
questions.translation: Điều gì vẫn còn kém phát triển?

questions.questionText: What are increasingly irrelevant?
questions.translation: Điều gì ngày càng trở nên không liên quan?

questions.questionText: What requires international coordination?
questions.translation: Điều gì yêu cầu phối hợp quốc tế?

questions.questionText: What will receive greater attention?
questions.translation: Điều gì sẽ nhận được sự quan tâm lớn hơn?

parts.title: Part 3: Discussion about urban economics and smart cities
parts.instructions: Listen to the discussion and answer questions 21-30.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 3. You will hear a discussion between an urban economist and a city planner about urban economics and smart cities. Look at questions 21 to 30. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 30.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: The economics of urbanization presents fascinating paradoxes. Cities generate remarkable agglomeration economies through knowledge spillovers, labor market pooling, and input sharing, yet they also create congestion, pollution, and spatial inequalities. The fundamental challenge is maximizing the benefits while minimizing the costs of density.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: This is precisely where smart city technologies offer transformative potential. IoT sensors, data analytics, and integrated mobility systems can optimize resource use, reduce congestion, and improve public service delivery. However, the economic benefits must be distributed equitably to avoid exacerbating existing inequalities.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: The land value capture mechanism is particularly important here. Public investments in transportation and amenities create substantial land value increases that are often captured privately. Better value capture could fund infrastructure while reducing speculative bubbles.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Digital infrastructure is becoming as crucial as physical infrastructure. Universal broadband access is now a prerequisite for economic participation. But we must ensure that smart city investments don't create digital divides or surveillance concerns.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: The governance dimension is critical. Smart cities require integrated planning across traditionally siloed departments. This demands new institutional capabilities and collaborative governance models that engage citizens as co-creators rather than passive recipients.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: The business models for smart city investments also need rethinking. Public-private partnerships can leverage expertise and funding, but they must be structured to protect public interests and ensure long-term sustainability rather than short-term profit maximization.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Urban economic resilience is another crucial consideration. Cities must diversify their economic bases to withstand shocks while maintaining the specialization that drives agglomeration benefits. This requires strategic economic development planning.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: The environmental economics of cities is equally important. Carbon pricing, circular economy principles, and green infrastructure can make cities more sustainable while creating economic opportunities. The cost of inaction far exceeds the cost of proactive investment.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Finally, we must consider the regional dimension. Successful cities can't exist as islands of prosperity surrounded by struggling hinterlands. Regional planning and fiscal transfers are essential for balanced development.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Ultimately, smart urban economics is about using technology, data, and innovative governance to create cities that are not only economically dynamic but also inclusive, sustainable, and resilient—cities that work for all their inhabitants.

questions.questionText: What paradox does urbanization present?
options: A) Benefits and costs of density
questions.translation: Đô thị hóa thể hiện nghịch lý gì?

questions.questionText: What do smart city technologies optimize?
questions.translation: Công nghệ thành phố thông minh tối ưu hóa điều gì?

questions.questionText: What mechanism is important for funding infrastructure?
questions.translation: Cơ chế nào quan trọng để tài trợ cho cơ sở hạ tầng?

questions.questionText: What is becoming as crucial as physical infrastructure?
questions.translation: Điều gì đang trở nên quan trọng như cơ sở hạ tầng vật chất?

questions.questionText: What do smart cities require across departments?
questions.translation: Thành phố thông minh yêu cầu điều gì giữa các phòng ban?

questions.questionText: What must public-private partnerships protect?
questions.translation: Quan hệ đối tác công-tư phải bảo vệ điều gì?

questions.questionText: What is crucial for urban economic resilience?
questions.translation: Điều gì quan trọng cho khả năng phục hồi kinh tế đô thị?

questions.questionText: What can make cities more sustainable?
questions.translation: Điều gì có thể làm cho các thành phố bền vững hơn?

questions.questionText: What is essential for balanced development?
questions.translation: Điều gì cần thiết cho phát triển cân bằng?

questions.questionText: What should cities work for?
questions.translation: Thành phố nên hoạt động vì ai?

parts.title: Part 4: Lecture on institutional economics and development
parts.instructions: Listen to the lecture and answer questions 31-40.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 4. You will hear a lecture on institutional economics and development. Look at questions 31 to 40. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Institutional economics provides crucial insights into the fundamental determinants of long-term economic development. The quality of institutions—defined as the formal and informal rules governing economic behavior—explains more of the variation in development outcomes than factor endowments or policy choices alone. Inclusive economic institutions that secure property rights, enforce contracts, and provide access to opportunity enable innovation and investment. Conversely, extractive institutions that concentrate power and opportunity in narrow elites stifle economic dynamism. The persistence of institutional arrangements presents a central puzzle: why do societies often maintain institutions that impede development? Historical institutionalism emphasizes the role of critical junctures and path dependence in shaping institutional trajectories. The distribution of political power fundamentally influences institutional development, as elites with vested interests in the status quo resist institutional reforms that would diminish their privileges. Building developmental states requires not just technical capacity but also political settlements that align elite interests with developmental objectives. The challenge of institutional reform lies in changing not just formal rules but also informal norms and enforcement mechanisms. Understanding these institutional dynamics is essential for designing effective development strategies that address the root causes of underdevelopment rather than merely treating symptoms.

questions.questionText: What provides insights into development determinants?
questions.translation: Điều gì cung cấp hiểu biết về các yếu tố quyết định phát triển?

questions.questionText: What explains development variation better?
questions.translation: Điều gì giải thích sự khác biệt phát triển tốt hơn?

questions.questionText: What do inclusive institutions enable?
questions.translation: Thể chế bao trùm cho phép điều gì?

questions.questionText: What do extractive institutions stifle?
questions.translation: Thể chế chiếm đoạt kìm hãm điều gì?

questions.questionText: What presents a central puzzle?
questions.translation: Điều gì đặt ra một câu đố trung tâm?

questions.questionText: What emphasizes critical junctures?
questions.translation: Điều gì nhấn mạnh các thời điểm quan trọng?

questions.questionText: What fundamentally influences institutional development?
questions.translation: Điều gì ảnh hưởng cơ bản đến phát triển thể chế?

questions.questionText: What do developmental states require?
questions.translation: Các nhà nước phát triển yêu cầu điều gì?

questions.questionText: What does institutional reform require changing?
questions.translation: Cải cách thể chế yêu cầu thay đổi điều gì?

questions.questionText: What is essential for effective development strategies?
questions.translation: Điều gì cần thiết cho các chiến lược phát triển hiệu quả?

passages.title: Passage 1: The Economics of Knowledge and Innovation Systems
passages.content: The economics of knowledge represents a fundamental shift in understanding the drivers of economic growth and development. Knowledge differs from conventional economic goods in its characteristics as a non-rival, partially excludable good with significant positive externalities. These characteristics create inherent market failures in knowledge production and diffusion, justifying public intervention through research funding, intellectual property rights, and innovation policies. National innovation systems conceptualize innovation as an interactive process involving multiple actors—firms, universities, research institutes, and government agencies—connected through networks and institutions. The absorptive capacity of firms and economies determines their ability to recognize, assimilate, and apply external knowledge, highlighting the importance of human capital and learning processes. The geography of innovation exhibits strong agglomeration effects, with knowledge spillovers decaying rapidly over distance, explaining the persistence of innovation clusters despite digital connectivity. The digital transformation has created new knowledge governance challenges related to data ownership, platform control, and artificial intelligence. Open science and open innovation models challenge traditional intellectual property regimes, emphasizing collaboration and knowledge sharing. The economics of artificial intelligence introduces novel considerations regarding the distribution of innovation rents, the impact on labor markets, and the governance of algorithmic decision-making. Understanding the knowledge economy requires analyzing not only the production of new knowledge but also its diffusion, absorption, and transformation into economic value through complex innovation ecosystems.

questions.questionText: What represents a fundamental shift in understanding growth?
What characteristics create market failures?
What conceptualizes innovation as interactive process?
What determines ability to apply external knowledge?
What exhibits strong agglomeration effects?
What has created new knowledge governance challenges?
What challenges traditional intellectual property?
What introduces novel considerations about innovation rents?
What requires analyzing diffusion and absorption?
What transforms knowledge into economic value?

passages.title: Passage 2: Behavioral Development Economics
passages.content: Behavioral development economics integrates insights from psychology and experimental economics to enhance understanding of economic behavior in developing contexts. Traditional development economics often assumed rational, self-interested actors with stable preferences, but behavioral economics reveals systematic deviations from these assumptions due to cognitive limitations, social preferences, and mental models. Poverty itself creates cognitive burdens that impair decision-making, creating behavioral poverty traps where limited attention and mental bandwidth constrain the ability to make optimal choices. Present bias leads to underinvestment in education, health, and productive assets, while loss aversion inhibits risk-taking necessary for entrepreneurship. Social preferences including fairness concerns and conditional cooperation influence collective action problems in community development. Mental models—deeply held beliefs about how the world works—shape economic behavior and can persist even when objectively inaccurate, affecting technology adoption, financial decisions, and occupational choices. Nudge-based interventions have demonstrated success in improving savings behavior, preventive healthcare, and educational outcomes by redesigning choice architectures. However, behavioral approaches face limitations in addressing structural constraints and power imbalances. The integration of behavioral insights with traditional development economics offers a more comprehensive understanding of poverty dynamics and more effective policy design. This requires context-specific experimentation and careful attention to ethical considerations regarding paternalism and autonomy. Behavioral development economics ultimately aims to create policies that work with human psychology as it is, rather than as traditional models assume it to be.

questions.questionText: What integrates insights from psychology?
What reveals systematic deviations from rationality?
What creates behavioral poverty traps?
What leads to underinvestment in education?
What influences collective action problems?
What shape economic behavior through beliefs?
What have demonstrated success in improving outcomes?
What do behavioral approaches face limitations in addressing?
What offers more comprehensive understanding?
What does behavioral economics aim to work with?
options: A) Human psychology as it is

passages.title: Passage 3: The Political Economy of Reform
passages.content: The political economy of reform examines why economically beneficial policies are often politically difficult to implement, focusing on the distributional conflicts, institutional constraints, and ideological factors that shape policy outcomes. Economic reforms typically create winners and losers, and potential losers often organize more effectively to block changes than potential winners organize to support them. This collective action problem explains the status quo bias in economic policymaking. Institutional veto points—where multiple actors must consent to policy changes—can enable minority interests to block reforms even when they would benefit the majority. Ideological frameworks and cognitive biases shape how actors perceive their interests and the costs and benefits of reform. The timing and sequencing of reforms crucially influence their political feasibility, with reforms often more achievable during crises when the status quo becomes untenable or during political transitions when old veto players are weakened. Compensation mechanisms that mitigate losses for powerful groups can facilitate reform implementation, though they may reduce economic efficiency. The credibility of reform commitments affects their economic impact, as private actors may not respond to announced reforms if they doubt their sustainability. International factors including conditional lending, technical assistance, and peer learning can influence domestic reform processes but cannot substitute for domestic ownership. Successful reform strategies typically combine technical soundness with political savvy, building coalitions for change while managing opposition through compensation, persuasion, and strategic sequencing. Understanding the political economy of reform is essential for designing implementable policies that achieve both economic efficiency and political sustainability.

questions.questionText: What examines why beneficial policies are difficult?
What problem explains status quo bias?
What can enable minority interests to block reforms?
What shape how actors perceive interests?
When are reforms often more achievable?
What can facilitate reform implementation?
What affects economic impact of reforms?
What cannot substitute for domestic ownership?
What do successful strategies combine?
options: A) Technical soundness and political savvy

questions.questionText: What is essential for designing implementable policies?

tasks.title: Writing Task 1
tasks.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on this task. The diagram below shows the circular economy model compared to the traditional linear economy. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.
tasks.description: You will be presented with a diagram showing economic models and asked to describe the information in your own words.

sampleData.title: Linear vs Circular Economy Models

Circular Economy: Design → Make → Use → Reuse/Repair/Refurbish → Recycle → Back to Make

sampleData.description: Comparison of traditional linear economic model with modern circular economy approach

tasks.title: Writing Task 2
tasks.instructions: You should spend about 40 minutes on this task. Some argue that artificial intelligence and automation will lead to widespread technological unemployment and increased inequality, while others believe they will create new job categories and boost productivity. Discuss both these views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.
tasks.description: You will be presented with contrasting views on AI and automation impacts and asked to discuss them.
keyPoints: Arguments about job displacement and inequality
Arguments about job creation and productivity

questions.questionText: _____ comprehensive the economic reforms, their success ultimately depends on institutional capacity and political will.
questions.translation: Dù các cải cách kinh tế có toàn diện đến đâu, thành công của chúng cuối cùng phụ thuộc vào năng lực thể chế và ý chí chính trị.

questions.questionText: The development strategy, _____ on extensive stakeholder consultation, demonstrated remarkable resilience during the economic crisis.
questions.explanation: Past participle in reduced relative clause
questions.translation: Chiến lược phát triển, dựa trên tham vấn rộng rãi các bên liên quan, đã thể hiện khả năng phục hồi đáng kể trong cuộc khủng hoảng kinh tế.

questions.questionText: So profound _____ that traditional economic indicators fail to capture its full impact.
options: A) has the digital transformation been
B) the digital transformation has been
questions.translation: Chuyển đổi kỹ thuật số sâu sắc đến mức các chỉ số kinh tế truyền thống không nắm bắt được tác động đầy đủ của nó.

questions.questionText: The policy committee recommended that environmental sustainability _____ at the core of all economic planning.
questions.translation: Ủy ban chính sách khuyến nghị rằng tính bền vững môi trường phải được đặt ở cốt lõi của tất cả kế hoạch kinh tế.

questions.questionText: _____ the complex interdependencies of global value chains, regional economic shocks can have worldwide repercussions.
questions.translation: Xét đến sự phụ thuộc lẫn nhau phức tạp của các chuỗi giá trị toàn cầu, các cú sốc kinh tế khu vực có thể có tác động toàn cầu.

questions.questionText: The innovation ecosystem, _____ numerous research institutions and startups, has become a model for regional development.
questions.explanation: Present participle in reduced relative clause
questions.translation: Hệ sinh thái đổi mới, bao gồm nhiều viện nghiên cứu và startup, đã trở thành mô hình cho phát triển khu vực.

questions.questionText: So rapidly _____ that regulatory frameworks struggle to keep pace.
questions.translation: Công nghệ tài chính phát triển nhanh đến mức các khuôn khổ quy định phải vật lộn để theo kịp.

questions.questionText: The board insisted that climate risk assessments _____ integrated into all investment decisions.
questions.translation: Hội đồng quản trị nhấn mạnh rằng đánh giá rủi ro khí hậu phải được tích hợp vào tất cả các quyết định đầu tư.

questions.questionText: _____ the prevailing economic uncertainties, innovation investment continues to demonstrate remarkable resilience.
questions.translation: Bất chấp những bất ổn kinh tế hiện hành, đầu tư đổi mới tiếp tục thể hiện khả năng phục hồi đáng chú ý.

questions.questionText: The economic partnership, _____ over a decade of negotiation, has transformed regional trade patterns.
questions.explanation: Past participle in reduced relative clause
questions.translation: Quan hệ đối tác kinh tế, được xây dựng qua hơn một thập kỷ đàm phán, đã biến đổi các mô hình thương mại khu vực.

questions.questionText: So significant _____ that it has redefined global economic governance.
options: A] has the shift in economic power been
B] the shift in economic power has been
C] was the shift in economic power
D] the shift in economic power was
questions.translation: Sự thay đổi quyền lực kinh tế quan trọng đến mức nó đã định nghĩa lại quản trị kinh tế toàn cầu.

questions.questionText: The committee demanded that transparency _____ in all public procurement processes.
questions.translation: Ủy ban yêu cầu rằng tính minh bạch phải được đảm bảo trong tất cả các quy trình mua sắm công.

questions.questionText: _____ the sophisticated economic modeling, predicting market behavior remains inherently uncertain.
questions.translation: Bất chấp mô hình hóa kinh tế tinh vi, dự đoán hành vi thị trường vẫn cố hữu không chắc chắn.

questions.questionText: The regulatory framework, _____ to address emerging digital risks, requires continuous updating.
questions.explanation: Past participle in reduced relative clause
questions.translation: Khuôn khổ quy định, được thiết kế để giải quyết các rủi ro kỹ thuật số mới nổi, yêu cầu cập nhật liên tục.

questions.questionText: So complex _____ that traditional policy tools have proven inadequate.
options: A] have modern economic challenges become
B] modern economic challenges have become
questions.translation: Các thách thức kinh tế hiện đại trở nên phức tạp đến mức các công cụ chính sách truyền thống đã chứng minh là không đầy đủ.

questions.questionText: The international agreement requires that environmental standards _____ uniformly applied.
questions.translation: Thỏa thuận quốc tế yêu cầu rằng các tiêu chuẩn môi trường phải được áp dụng thống nhất.

questions.questionText: _____ the detailed economic analysis, implementation challenges persisted.
questions.translation: Bất chấp phân tích kinh tế chi tiết, các thách thức triển khai vẫn tồn tại.

questions.questionText: The development model, _____ on inclusive growth principles, has achieved remarkable poverty reduction.
questions.explanation: Past participle in reduced relative clause
questions.translation: Mô hình phát triển, dựa trên các nguyên tắc tăng trưởng bao trùm, đã đạt được thành tựu giảm nghèo đáng kể.

questions.questionText: So rapidly _____ that traditional industries have struggled to adapt.
options: A] is the technological landscape changing
B] the technological landscape is changing
C] has the technological landscape changed
D] the technological landscape has changed
questions.translation: Bối cảnh công nghệ thay đổi nhanh đến mức các ngành công nghiệp truyền thống phải vật lộn để thích nghi.

questions.questionText: The reform package mandates that anti-corruption measures _____ strengthened across all government agencies.
questions.translation: Gói cải cách yêu cầu rằng các biện pháp chống tham nhũng phải được tăng cường trên tất cả các cơ quan chính phủ.

metadata.author: IELTS Practice System
metadata.notes: IELTS Level 4.6 advanced economics test covering Listening (4 parts, 40 questions), Reading (3 passages, 30 questions), Writing (2 tasks), and Grammar (20 questions). Enhanced difficulty with sophisticated economic concepts including development paradigms, platform economics, urban economics, and institutional economics.
